articles.title: Port React Compiler to Rust
articles.summary: Port React Compiler to Rust by josephsavona · Pull Request #36173 · react/react · GitHub The port of the React Compiler to Rust is a work-in-progress, sharing early results with partners in parallel with continued development. Key points include heavy guidance from humans but majority coding by AI, public API based on "Rust Babel AST" + "Scope Info", and use of Rust's intermediate representation (HIR) for conversion between ASTs and native representations. The compiler is 3x faster when operating as a Babel plugin, with high serialization costs but significant optimization opportunities.

articles.title: Rich Sutton on AI creativity and discovery
articles.summary: Rich Sutton, a renowned expert in artificial intelligence, discusses his research on how AI can facilitate creative problem-solving. He argues that current AI systems lack the ability to truly discover new solutions, relying instead on existing knowledge and patterns. Sutton proposes an alternative approach, where AI is used to augment human creativity by identifying novel connections between seemingly unrelated concepts. By leveraging this hybrid approach, developers can unlock new possibilities for innovation and discovery in fields such as computer vision and natural language processing.

articles.title: CVE-2026-45447: Heap Use-After-Free in the OpenSSL PKCS7_verify() Function
articles.summary: TheCVE-2026-45447 vulnerability affects the OpenSSL library, specifically its PKCS7_verify() function, which can cause a heap buffer over-read in certain scenarios. This issue is particularly concerning on 64-bit Unix and Unix-like platforms where it may lead to Denial of Service or memory corruption. The vulnerability is caused by an integer truncation mishandling when the content length of an ASN.1 primitive element exceeds two gigabytes, potentially leading to a crash or data corruption in applications that use d2i_X509(), d2i_PKCS7(), or other decoding functions.

articles.title: CSS: Unavoidable Bad Parts

Modern web development has a relatively small subset of CSS that can be learned for simple tasks, but there are many gotchas and bad practices that can lead to frustrating debugging experiences. HTML5 semantic tag names like `main`, `article`, and `nav` make it easier to structure pages, while the lack of explicit list elements like `ul` and `dl` makes it harder to implement layouts. Additionally, CSS layout is a struggle in many GUI frameworks, with different systems using varying heuristics to arrange boxes on the screen.
